content:
# Genesis Chapter 45 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 26v–27r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_045_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_045_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE — LXX | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0049 (page 45), https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0050 (page 46)

---

## "I Am Joseph"

**1** Joseph could not control himself any longer in front of all those who were standing near him, and he cried out, "Everyone leave me!" So there was no one with him when Joseph made himself known to his brothers. **2** He burst into weeping, and the Egyptians heard it, and the household of Pharaoh heard it.

**3** Joseph said to his brothers, "I am Joseph! Is my father still alive?" But his brothers could not answer him — they were paralyzed with terror before him.

---

## Come Close

**4** Joseph said to his brothers, "Please — come close to me." And they came close. He said, "I am Joseph, your brother — the one you sold into Egypt. **5** And now, do not be distressed, and do not be angry with yourselves for selling me here — because God sent me ahead of you to preserve life. **6** For the famine has been in the land these two years, and there are still five more years to come in which there will be neither plowing nor harvest. **7** But God sent me ahead of you to establish a remnant for you on the earth, and to keep you alive as a great band of survivors. **8** So it was not you who sent me here — it was God. He has made me a father to Pharaoh, lord over all his household, and ruler over the entire land of Egypt.

---

## Go Up to My Father

**9** "Hurry — go up to my father and say to him, 'This is what your son Joseph says: God has made me lord over all Egypt. Come down to me — do not delay. **10** You will live in the land of Goshen and be near me — you and your children and your grandchildren, your flocks and your herds and everything you have. **11** I will provide for you there — for there are still five years of famine to come — so that you and your household and everything you have will not fall into poverty.'

**12** "You can see with your own eyes — and my brother Benjamin can see — that it is my own mouth speaking to you. **13** Tell my father about all my glory in Egypt, and all that you have seen. And hurry — bring my father down here."

---

## The Weeping

**14** Then he fell on his brother Benjamin's neck and wept, and Benjamin wept on his neck. **15** He kissed all his brothers and wept over them — and after that his brothers spoke with him.

---

## Pharaoh's Welcome

**16** The report reached Pharaoh's house: "Joseph's brothers have come." Pharaoh was pleased, and so was all his court. **17** Pharaoh said to Joseph, "Say to your brothers, 'Do this: load your animals and go — travel back to the land of Canaan, **18** and take your father and your households and come to me. I will give you the best of the land of Egypt, and you will eat the richness of the land.' **19** You are instructed: tell them, 'Do this: take wagons from the land of Egypt for your little ones and for your wives, and bring your father, and come. **20** Do not be concerned about your belongings — for the best of all the land of Egypt is yours.'"

---

## The Provisions

**21** And the sons of Israel did so. Joseph gave them wagons, as Pharaoh had ordered, and he gave them provisions for the journey. **22** He gave each of them a change of clothes — but to Benjamin he gave three hundred pieces of silver and five changes of clothes. **23** And to his father he sent this: ten donkeys loaded with the best things from Egypt, and ten female donkeys loaded with grain and bread and provisions for his father for the journey.

**24** Then he sent his brothers off, and they went. He said to them, "Do not quarrel on the way."

---

## Jacob's Spirit Revives

**25** They went up from Egypt and came to the land of Canaan, to their father Jacob. **26** They told him, "Joseph is still alive — and he is ruler over all the land of Egypt." And Jacob's heart went cold, for he did not believe them.

**27** But they told him all the words Joseph had spoken to them, and he saw the wagons Joseph had sent to carry him. And the spirit of their father Jacob came back to life. **28** "Enough!" said Israel. "My son Joseph is still alive! I will go and see him before I die."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Lo yakol l'hit'appeq — the shattering of the mask

**וְלֹא-יָכֹל יוֹסֵף לְהִתְאַפֵּק** — "and Joseph could not control himself" — *velo yakol Yosef l'hit'appeq*.

The verb **אָפַק** (*apaq*, in the Hitpael: to restrain oneself, to hold oneself in, to control oneself) has appeared once before in the Joseph narrative: in Genesis 43:31, after Joseph saw Benjamin and was about to weep — *vayit'appeq*, "and he controlled himself." The same root, the same struggle. In chapter 43, he managed it: he went to a private room, wept there, washed his face, came out, composed himself, and ordered the food. He held the mask of the Egyptian official firmly in place.

Here, in 45:1, the word appears in a negative construction: *lo yakol* — "he was not able." The control that was barely achieved in chapter 43 is now impossible. The hitpael's reflexive force is precise: Joseph cannot keep performing the action *upon himself* of holding himself in. Judah's speech has broken something open that cannot be re-closed. The man who staged an elaborate seven-chapter test, who has maintained a dramatic fiction across multiple encounters, who has wept in secret twice already — this man cannot manage one more moment of control.

The verse says he cried out (*vayyiqra'*) to everyone standing near him: *hotsi'u kol-ish me'alay* — "get every man out from around me." He clears the room. What is about to happen is not for Egyptian eyes. It is not even for the narrator to mediate; the narrator simply reports that no one else was there when Joseph revealed himself to his brothers. The disclosure is private in the largest possible sense: it happens in a room suddenly emptied of everyone except the twelve sons of Jacob.

---

### Ani Yosef — the two words that change everything

**אֲנִי יוֹסֵף** — "I am Joseph."

Two Hebrew words. The simplest grammatical construction in the language: the personal pronoun and the name. No verb, no elaboration, no preamble — simply: *I am Joseph.*

The last time the brothers heard the name Yosef spoken with this kind of directness was in Genesis 37, when they threw him in the pit and sold him. Since then, in the entire narrative of chapters 37–44, the name "Joseph" appears regularly in the narrator's discourse and in Jacob's speeches, but never in the mouth of the Egyptian official the brothers have been standing before. That official has asked about their family, has tested them, has wept in hidden rooms, has accused them, has offered them exits — but he has never said his own name. Now he does.

The brothers' response — **וְלֹא-יָכְלוּ אֶחָיו לַעֲנוֹת אֹתוֹ כִּי נִבְהֲלוּ מִפָּנָיו** (*velo yakhlu echav la'anot oto ki nibhalu mipanav*, "and his brothers could not answer him, for they were dismayed before him") — is among the most emotionally exact observations in the entire Joseph story. The verb **בָּהַל** (*bahal*, to be alarmed, terrified, dismayed, thrown into confusion) describes not simple fear but the specific mental state of being suddenly shattered by the unexpected. Their minds cannot assemble a response. Every assumption they have operated under for twenty-plus years — Joseph is dead, Joseph is gone, we sold a boy to Ishmaelites and he disappeared into Egypt and that is the end of it — has collapsed in two words.

And then, immediately: "Is my father still alive?" (*ha'od avi chai*) — his first question after the revelation is not about himself, not about what they did, not about the decades of suffering. It is about the father. The reader has been told in chapter 44 that Jacob is alive; Joseph has been told. But the question — spoken as the first thing out of his mouth after "I am Joseph" — reveals where Joseph's heart is. His own suffering is not the first thing in the room. His father is.

---

### The providence theology — "God sent me"

**וַיִּשְׁלָחֵנִי אֱלֹהִים לִפְנֵיכֶם לִשְׁמֹר לָכֶם שְׁאֵרִית בָּאָרֶץ** — "God sent me ahead of you to establish a remnant for you on the earth."

Verses 5–8 contain the theological heart of the entire Joseph narrative, and possibly the most concise statement of providence theology in the book of Genesis. Joseph says the same essential thing three times, in ascending clarity:

**V.5:** *ki l'michyah shelachani Elohim lifneikhem* — "because God sent me ahead of you to preserve life." The word **מִחְיָה** (*michyah*) means life, preservation of life, the act of keeping alive — it is related to the verb *chayah*, to live. The purpose of the sending is life. Not Joseph's benefit, not Egyptian politics, not Pharaoh's enrichment — *life*.

**V.7:** *vayyishlach Elohim lifneikhem... lehavriach lachem she'erit ba'aretz* — "God sent me before you to establish a remnant for you on the earth." The word **שְׁאֵרִית** (*she'erit*, remnant, remainder, surviving portion) is a term that will carry enormous weight in the prophetic literature — the "remnant of Israel" that survives judgment is the seed of the new beginning. Here Joseph uses it for his brothers and their households: you are the remnant, I am here to keep you as a remnant in existence.

**V.8:** *lo attem shelachtem oti hennah ki ha-Elohim* — "it was not you who sent me here, but God." The double negation and positive assertion structurally parallel a Hebrew *rib* (lawsuit): *not you... but God*. Joseph is not saying: it doesn't matter that you sold me. He is saying: your action and God's action are not the same action, and when I trace the causal chain back, the origin is God. **הִנֵּנִי לְפַרְעֹה לְאָב** (*hineni l'Far'o l'av*, "behold me as a father to Pharaoh") — the word **אָב** (*av*, father) applied to a relationship with Pharaoh signals an authority not of domination but of wise guidance and provision, the father-figure of counsel.

This is not naïveté about the brothers' guilt. It is a specific and mature theological claim: that the evil done to him has been overarched by a divine purpose that the brothers did not intend and could not have foreseen. The word **שְׁלַח** (*shalach*, to send) appears in all three formulations — God *sent* me — insisting that the initiative behind the whole sequence belongs to God. Joseph has had years in prison and in power to arrive at this reading of his own life. He is not improvising it now; he is articulating what he has concluded.

---

### "Do not be angry with yourselves" — the generosity of the revelation

**וְעַתָּה אַל-תֵּעָצְבוּ וְאַל-יִחַר בְּעֵינֵיכֶם כִּי-מְכַרְתֶּם אֹתִי הֵנָּה** — "And now, do not be distressed and do not be angry with yourselves for selling me here."

The verb **עָצַב** (*atsav*, to be pained, grieved, distressed) in the Niphal here describes the inward pain of self-reproach. Joseph is not telling them their guilt did not matter — he has just said *you sold me here* (*mechartem oti henna*, "you sold me to this place"), a plain acknowledgment of the historical fact and its moral meaning. But his first pastoral word after the revelation is: do not do that to yourselves. The same compassion that made him weep in private when he saw their suffering, the same compassion that made him ask after his father's welfare, now extends immediately to the brothers who did what they did to him. He is not performing forgiveness; he is actively interrupting their imminent self-destruction.

**וְאַל-יִחַר בְּעֵינֵיכֶם** (*ve'al yichar be'eineikhem*, "do not let it burn in your eyes") is a striking idiom: anger as a burning in the eyes, the eyes as the place where self-directed fury ignites. Joseph is watching the brothers from close range — he has called them *closer* to him in verse 4, *gesha na elai* — and he can see what is happening in their faces. He interrupts it before it begins to consume them.

---

### Goshen — proximity as the shape of restoration

**וְיָשַׁבְתָּ בְאֶרֶץ-גֹּשֶׁן וְהָיִיתָ קָרוֹב אֵלַי** — "You will live in the land of Goshen and be near me."

The phrase **קָרוֹב אֵלַי** (*qarov elai*, "near me") is small and easily passed over, but it carries everything. After twenty-plus years of separation — father from son, brother from brother — the restoration Joseph envisions is not primarily one of comfort or safety or provision, though he promises all of those. It is spatial nearness. You will be *near me*. The land of Goshen in the eastern Nile delta was fertile pastureland, good for flocks — practical reasons for the placement exist — but Joseph names the relational dimension before the practical one. *Near me.* The goal of the long chain of events — famine, descent, tests, revelation — is not merely survival. It is the reconstitution of nearness.

The list that follows in verse 10 — *you and your children and your grandchildren, your flocks and your herds and everything you have* — extends the nearness in concentric circles: persons, then animals, then possessions. The whole household, entire. Nothing left in Canaan, nothing left to mourn.

---

### Chalifot semalot — garments given back

**וְלָכֻלָּם נָתַן לָאִישׁ חֲלִפֹת שְׂמָלֹת** — "To each of them he gave a change of clothes."

**חֲלִפוֹת שְׂמָלוֹת** (*chalifot semalot*, "changes of garments, changes of clothes") — the garment motif that has run like a dark thread through the entire Joseph narrative returns here, transformed.

The garments in this story have all been instruments of destruction. Joseph's *ketonet passim* (the coat of many colors, or of long sleeves) was stripped from him by his brothers in Genesis 37:23 and used as evidence of his pretend death. Joseph's cloak was grabbed by Potiphar's wife and used as evidence of his pretend assault in Genesis 39:12-18. The brothers' own garments were torn in grief in Genesis 44:13. Garments have been stripped, seized, fabricated, torn — every garment in this story has carried grief.

Now Joseph gives garments. The word **חֲלִפוֹת** (*chalifot*, changes) comes from the root **חָלַף** (*chalaf*, to pass on, to change, to renew — used of the renewing of strength in Isaiah 40:31, *yachalifū koach*, "they will renew their strength"). The changes of garments are not luxury items; they are a symbol of renewal, of a life that has enough to change out of and into. To have garments to change into is to not be stripped, to not be left naked or in mourning clothes. Joseph is returning to his brothers — in material, tangible, wearable form — what was taken from all of them when garments were the instrument of betrayal and pretend death.

And to Benjamin, the special portion: five changes and three hundred silver. The five-fold echo of the fivefold portion at the dinner in chapter 43 is not accidental. Benjamin remains the beloved youngest, and the number five has become the Joseph narrative's number for excess grace given to the one who is loved.

---

### Al-tirgezu — the one parting instruction

**וַיְשַׁלַּח אֶת-אֶחָיו וַיֵּלֵכוּ וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם אַל-תִּרְגְּזוּ בַּדָּרֶךְ** — "He sent his brothers off and they went, and he said to them, 'Do not quarrel on the way.'"

The verb **רָגַז** (*ragaz*) covers a remarkable range: to tremble, to quake, to be agitated, to be disturbed, and — in relational contexts — to quarrel, to be in uproar. It is used of the earth shaking (2 Samuel 22:8), of nations trembling in fear (Exodus 15:14), and of human agitation and conflict. The traditional rendering "do not quarrel" captures the relational dimension; "do not be troubled/agitated" captures the psychological. Joseph may mean both simultaneously.

The brothers are now carrying the most explosive information anyone in Canaan has ever received: *Joseph is alive, he is ruler of Egypt, and he forgives us.* They are carrying this on a long journey home. Joseph knows his brothers. He knows what happens in the space of a caravan between people who share a guilty past and a history of deflecting guilt onto one another. He knows — he has just heard Judah rehearse it all — what the accusations and counter-accusations of Genesis 37 still taste like after twenty years. *Go home. Tell your father. Do not spend the journey tearing each other apart over who is responsible for what.*

It is perhaps the most intimate thing Joseph says in the entire chapter — more intimate than the weeping, more intimate than the theology. It is the word of a man who knows his family precisely, who has forgiven them but is not naive about what they are capable of doing to each other, and who is simply asking them to hold together long enough to reach their father.

---

### Vattechi ruach Ya'akov — the spirit that comes back to life

**וַתְּחִי רוּחַ יַעֲקֹב אֲבִיהֶם** — "And the spirit of their father Jacob came back to life."

The verb **חָיָה** (*chayah*, to live, to be alive) in the Qal with *ruach* (spirit, breath, wind) as its subject: the spirit *lived*. In Jacob's case, more than lived: came back to life, revived, was renewed. The implication of the clause is that the spirit has been functionally dead — not that Jacob was unconscious or literally dying, but that something essential in him had been extinguished. The years since Genesis 37, since the coat came back soaked in blood and he concluded that Joseph had been torn to pieces — those years have been years of a man whose spirit is present only in its diminished, grieving form.

The sequence of Jacob's response in verses 26–28 is precise: (1) his heart went cold (*vayafog libbo*, his heart became numb, grew cold, lost sensation — *pug* means to grow cold, to be numb, frozen), because he did not believe them; (2) they told him all Joseph's words and showed him the wagons; (3) the spirit revived. The revival comes not from the words alone but from the physical evidence: *he saw the wagons Joseph had sent to carry him* (*vayar et ha'agalot*). The wagons are the material proof. Egyptian wagons don't come from Canaan. The story is true. Joseph is alive.

Then in verse 28 the name shifts: **וַיֹּאמֶר יִשְׂרָאֵל** (*vayomer Yisrael*, "and Israel said"). The patriarch is addressed as Jacob through the numbness and the revival, and then — in the moment of decision, the moment of declaration — he speaks as Israel. **רַב** (*rav*, "enough, much, great") — the first word out of his mouth is not a sentence but an exclamation, a single word that the NRSV renders "it is enough!" The word is doing triple duty: *enough of grief, enough of disbelieving, enough of living as if Joseph were dead.* My son Joseph is still alive. I will go and see him before I die.

The last time Jacob made a journey-declaration of this kind, it was in Genesis 46:30, where he will say *now let me die, since I have seen your face* — the same father-son reunion logic. Here, at the announcement, the declaration is directional and volitional: **אֵלְכָה** (*elechah*, "I will go") — the cohortative of *halakh*, expressing strong resolve. *I am going.* Not "I hope to go," not "perhaps we should go." I am going. The spirit that revived in verse 27 speaks in verse 28, and it speaks as Israel, and it speaks in the cohortative. The patriarch who was barely alive is now making plans.

---

### Structure — revelation, mission, reunion, revival

Genesis 45 has a beautifully clear four-movement structure that mirrors and inverts the four movements of the Joseph story's descent:

**Movement 1 — The revelation (vv.1-3):** Control breaks. Joseph empties the room. "I am Joseph." Silence. Terror. Then the first question: is my father alive?

**Movement 2 — The providence speech (vv.4-8):** Come close. I am Joseph your brother. Do not be distressed. God sent me. Three times: God sent me. The theological reframing of twenty years of suffering.

**Movement 3 — The mission and the provisions (vv.9-24):** Go back. Tell the father. Bring the whole household. Goshen. Provision for the journey. The garments given back. The one parting word: do not quarrel on the way.

**Movement 4 — The revival (vv.25-28):** The brothers ascend to Canaan. The impossible news. The numb heart. The evidence of the wagons. The spirit that comes back to life. Israel speaks: I will go and see him before I die.

The entire chapter is the pivot of the Joseph story: the long downward movement (betrayal, slavery, false accusation, imprisonment) meets the long upward movement (wisdom, promotion, provision, revelation) at this hinge. Everything that follows — the descent to Egypt, the reunion, the blessing — flows from this moment when Joseph could not control himself any longer and cried out the two words his brothers had not heard in twenty years: *ani Yosef.*

---

### Manuscript observations

The Leningrad Codex folio 26v (GEN_045_page_001.jpg) begins its right column with the final lines of Genesis 44 — Judah's closing question visible in the opening lines — and transitions immediately into Genesis 45 without a petuhah (open section) break at the chapter boundary, consistent with the narrative's dramatic refusal to pause between Judah's question and Joseph's breaking. The three-column layout of the Leningrad Codex is clearly visible, with the characteristic Tiberian pointing above and below each consonantal line.

The opening of Genesis 45 — **וְלֹא-יָכֹל יוֹסֵף לְהִתְאַפֵּק** (*velo yakol Yosef l'hit'appeq*, v.1) — is legible in the right column of folio 26v with the characteristic hitpael prefix *hit-* on the root **א-פ-ק** (*aleph-pe-qof*), pointed with the expected Tiberian vowels for the hitpael infinitive construct. The negative **לֹא** (*lo*) and the verb **יָכֹל** (*yakol*, was able) precede it, the whole phrase flowing in the characteristic compressed style of the Leningrad Codex narrative portions.

The great revelation — **אֲנִי יוֹסֵף** (*ani Yosef*, v.3) — is visible in the Codex as the brief, emphatic two-word clause that it is in the Hebrew, its brevity standing out against the surrounding longer clauses. The question that immediately follows — **הַעוֹד אָבִי חָי** (*ha'od avi chai*, "is my father still alive?") — is legible in the characteristic *hey*-interrogative opening.

The threefold **שָׁלַח** (*shalach*, to send) theology of verses 5, 7, and 8 is traceable in the folio with the root **שׁ-ל-ח** (*shin-lamed-chet*) recurring in the central column of the folio as the providential claim is articulated and rearticulated.

Folio 27r (GEN_045_page_002.jpg) carries the second half of chapter 45 from approximately verse 16 — Pharaoh's welcome — through verse 28 and into the opening of Genesis 46. The Masoretic *parasha* boundary markers (petuhot and setumot) that divide the larger sections of the chapter are visible at the transitions between major movements. The final verse — **וַיֹּאמֶר יִשְׂרָאֵל רַב עוֹד-יוֹסֵף בְּנִי חָי אֵלְכָה** (*vayomer Yisrael rav, od Yosef beni chai, elechah*, "and Israel said: enough! My son Joseph is still alive! I will go") — falls near the end of the central column of folio 27r with the name-shift from Ya'akov (v.27) to Yisrael (v.28) visible and the cohortative **אֵלְכָה** (*elechah*, "I will go") concluding the chapter in its characteristic emphatic form.

Notable LXX (Vaticanus) variants for Genesis 45:

**V.1** — For **לְהִתְאַפֵּק** (*l'hit'appeq*, "to control himself"), Vaticanus page pa_0045 reads **ἀντέχεσθαι** (*antechesthai*, "to hold out against, to contain oneself, to withstand"), a compound of **ἀντί** (*anti*) + **ἔχω** (*echō*, to hold). The LXX renders the hitpael reflexive of containing oneself through the Greek compound that conveys active resistance against something — *holding out against* the emotion rather than the Hebrew's *restraining himself inwardly*. The Greek captures the sense of active internal struggle well, though the reflexive nuance of the hitpael (he could not perform the action *upon himself*) is slightly externalized in the Greek: the LXX reads more as *he could not withstand* (the pressure from outside or within) than *he could not hold himself in*.

**V.5** — For **לִשְׁמֹר לָכֶם שְׁאֵרִית** (*lishmor lachem she'erit*, "to preserve for you a remnant"), Vaticanus reads **ἵνα ὑπολίπησθε ὑμῶν κατάλειμμα ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς** — "so that you might have a remnant left upon the earth." The Greek **κατάλειμμα** (*kataleimma*, remnant, what is left behind) renders **שְׁאֵרִית** (*she'erit*) accurately; both words carry the sense of a surviving portion after devastation. The LXX uses a purpose clause where the Hebrew uses an infinitive of purpose, preserving the theological intent: Joseph was sent *in order that* a remnant would survive.

**V.8** — For **לְפַרְעֹה לְאָב** (*l'Far'o l'av*, "to Pharaoh as a father"), Vaticanus reads **πατέρα Φαραω** (*patera Pharaō*, "father of Pharaoh"), construing the phrase as "father *of* Pharaoh" rather than "father *to* Pharaoh." This is a minor but interesting syntactic difference: the Hebrew *l'av* (with the lamed of *lamad* denoting "as, serving as, in the capacity of a father") can be read either as "a father figure to Pharaoh" (adviser, guide, wise counselor — the Egyptian title used for high officials) or as "father of Pharaoh" in the sense of progenitor of power. The LXX opts for the genitive construction, which in Egyptian court context fits the honorific title **it-ntr** ("divine father") used for senior advisors and viziers. The Vaticanus reading may preserve an understanding of the title closer to Egyptian administrative conventions than the more relational English "father to Pharaoh."

**V.12** — For **כִּי-פִי הַמְדַבֵּר אֲלֵיכֶם** (*ki fi hammedabber aleikhem*, "that it is my own mouth speaking to you"), Vaticanus reads **ὅτι τὸ στόμα μου τὸ λαλοῦν πρὸς ὑμᾶς** — "that my mouth is the one speaking to you." The Greek is essentially a word-for-word rendering of the Hebrew, preserving the emphasis of Joseph's identification proof: not only do you hear an Egyptian official saying "I am Joseph," you are seeing the mouth that speaks these words and can verify that it is speaking in Hebrew (*nivrit*, in the brothers' own tongue — implicitly). The identity claim in verse 12 is not merely spoken; it is physical and auditory. *This mouth, my mouth, speaking to you* — the emphasis on direct experience rather than report.

**V.26** — For **וַיָּפָג לִבּוֹ** (*vayafog libbo*, "and his heart grew cold/numb"), Vaticanus reads **ἐξέστη δὲ ἡ διάνοια αὐτοῦ** — "and his mind was astonished/stood outside itself." The Greek **ἐξίσταμαι** (*existamai*, to stand outside oneself, to be beside oneself, to be amazed — the root of our English "ecstasy") renders the Hebrew **פּוּג** (*pug*, to be numb, cold, frozen with shock) with a different image: where the Hebrew describes the freezing of the heart — the organ of will and decision going cold, becoming unresponsive — the Greek describes the mind standing outside itself in astonishment. Both describe the same psychological crisis of disbelief and shock. The Hebrew image is of *freezing*; the Greek image is of *displacement*. The Vaticanus perhaps softens the pathos slightly — a frozen heart is more desolate than an astonished mind — but the emotional reality is faithfully conveyed.

The Vaticanus pages covering Genesis 45 (pa_0045–pa_0046, https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0049 and https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0050) present the chapter in the characteristic three-column uncial layout. Page pa_0045 carries chapters 44–45 across the transition, with the chapter marker **CAP L** (chapter 50 in the Vaticanus internal numbering, not the modern chapter 45) visible near the top of the page. The revelation narrative, the providence speech, and the provisions section are all legible in the continuous uncial script. Page pa_0046 carries the latter portion of chapter 45 into the opening of chapter 46, with the Jacob revival narrative in the center column and the beginning of the descent to Egypt visible in the right column.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 26v–27r, West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Folio 26v (GEN_045_page_001.jpg, PDF page 51) carries the end of Genesis 44 and Genesis 45:1–approximately 45:15. Folio 27r (GEN_045_page_002.jpg, PDF page 52) carries Genesis 45:16–45:28 and the beginning of Genesis 46. Secondary reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, pages 45–46 (pa_0045–pa_0046); accessible at https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0049 and https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0050.*
